messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token needs a full
large-model forward pass; how can a small draft model propose
tokens in parallel that the large model verifies without quality
loss?


### Proposed method
We propose Parallel Draft–Verify Decoding (PDVD): a small draft model D proposes a block of the next B tokens in parallel, and the large target model L verifies and commits a maximal prefix of that block, guaranteeing the exact output that L would have produced under the chosen decoding policy (greedy or stochastic). At each step, we maintain L’s KV cache for the accepted prefix. D is a lightweight transformer trained to approximate L’s next-token distribution and, crucially, to predict the next B positions simultaneously using a lookahead masking scheme so its B predictions can be produced in one pass.

Drafting: Given prefix x1:t, we append B masked slots and run D once with a position-specific lookahead mask that prevents the masked slots from attending to each other, letting every future position see the same prefix but not cross-talk with other future positions. D outputs logits (or top-m lists) for positions t+1..t+B in parallel; we sample or select candidates from these logits according to the downstream decoding policy (e.g., greedy or temperature sampling) to form a draft block ŷ1:B.

Verification: We run L autoregressively over the draft block reusing the KV cache, but stop as soon as we encounter the first position j where D’s draft and L’s policy disagree. For greedy decoding, we accept all ŷ1:(j−1) that match L and set yj to L’s argmax; for stochastic decoding, we perform acceptance–rejection at each position i using the standard probability ratio test min(1, pL(yi|x, y< i)/pD(yi|x, y< i)), which yields exact samples from L. To save compute when a mismatch is likely, we add a shallow verifier gate g that inspects early-layer activations and D’s logits to predict rejection; if g flags a likely mismatch at position i, we abort the deep layers for that position and mark it as a rejection early, while still running the full L pass for any token we finally commit—preserving exactness.

Scheduling and pipelining: While L verifies the current block, D immediately drafts the next block from the (temporarily) extended context, keeping both models busy. Across a batch of sequences, we verify blocks in a strided, GPU-friendly manner. To further increase parallelism without changing correctness, maintain a cache of tentative tokens across future steps and iterate in sweeps: each sweep lets L verify only its assigned positions (earliest unverified positions across requests) using cached guesses elsewhere; after the sweep, we write back accepted tokens and repeat. This converges to the same sequence as fully sequential decoding, yet enables high hardware utilization via batched verification.

Training D and the verifier gate: Train D via distillation from L on next-token prediction and a blockwise objective: for randomly chosen prefixes, predict the next B tokens with the lookahead mask so every position learns to rely solely on the prefix. Train the shallow gate g to predict “accept vs reject” using features from early L layers and D’s logits, with a loss that heavily penalizes false accepts; at inference, g only short-circuits expensive verification on tokens that would be rejected, never on tokens we ultimately commit.

### Rationale
This design eliminates quality loss by committing tokens only after L has validated them, and by using exact acceptance–rejection for stochastic decoding. The small D amortizes the expensive large-model passes: if the average accepted-prefix length per draft is r·B (r = acceptance rate), then L performs one forward sweep per r·B emitted tokens instead of per token, yielding a speedup of roughly 1/r in the large-model compute, while D’s parallel draft is cheap and overlapped. The verifier gate further reduces wasted large-model work by aborting deep-layer computation early on blocks that will be rejected, without changing any committed outputs.

The lookahead masking allows D to produce multiple future positions in a single pass without causal leakage between those positions, which is what makes parallel drafting both valid and efficient. The iterative, cache-based scheduling maintains high device utilization and mirrors a fixed-point view of sequential decoding: multiple tentative positions are updated and corrected until they match L’s true trajectory, but tokens are only emitted once L has fully verified them—preserving exact equivalence to L’s outputs.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] LLM in a Flash: Efficient Large Language Model Inference with Limited Memory (2312.11514)
   problem: On-device LLM inference is constrained when model parameters exceed available DRAM; naively reloading large weights from flash each token is dominated by flash bandwidth/latency, making it too slow.
   method:  Store full model weights on flash and keep only embeddings and attention weights resident in DRAM to minimize repeated transfers. Exploit FFN activation sparsity by training a small low-rank predictor that, using only the current layer’s attention outputs, anticipates which intermediate neurons will be active after ReLU; then load only the corresponding FFN up/down weights from flash. This selective persistence and predictor-guided loading substantially reduces flash→DRAM traffic with marginal impact on zero-shot accuracy. (Evidence: selective persistence and sparsity use: lines 74–79, 76–77, predictor design and training: line 80, accuracy impact: lines 88–97.)

2. [IR_REC] Leveraging LLM Reasoning Enhances Personalized Recommender Systems (2408.00802)
   problem: Running large LLMs with CoT at inference is expensive; smaller models often lack reasoning ability for personalized recommendation tasks.
   method:  Distill reasoning from a stronger LLM by sampling multiple diverse reasoning–rating pairs per user–item at different decoding temperatures, then fine-tune smaller sequence-to-sequence models (e.g., Flan-T5) to generate reasoning followed by the rating using these sampled reasons plus gold rating labels as targets. Optional filters compare the large model’s predicted ratings to ground truth (5-class, binary, 1-off). Fine-tuning with reasoning consistently outperforms rating-only training, scales with model size, benefits from multiple reasoning paths, and shows domain-dependent effects of filtering.

3. [IR_REC] RetroMAE: Pre-Training Retrieval-oriented Language Models Via Masked Auto-Encoder (2205.12035)
   problem: Standard decoding in AE/MLM pre-training derives loss only from masked positions and uses a single, fixed context per token, under-utilizing training data and limiting signal diversity.
   method:  RetroMAE proposes enhanced decoding based on two-stream self-attention and a position-specific attention mask. It builds query (H1) and context (H2) streams conditioned on the sentence embedding and applies a position-specific mask matrix that always masks each token’s own position (diagonal) while exposing sampled visible tokens, enabling every token to be reconstructed and each to use a unique context. With a single-layer decoder, this yields 100% token utilization and diversified contexts, improving data efficiency and retrieval quality (e.g., MSMARCO MRR@10 0.3553 vs. 0.3462; NQ MRR@10 0.7704 vs. 0.7562 in ablations).

4. [MULTIMODAL] End-to-End Transformer Based Model for Image Captioning (2203.15350)
   problem: In Transformer-based captioning, vision–language interaction typically occurs only at cross-attention, leaving the decoder without global visual context early in processing, which weakens reasoning over the image.
   method:  PureT introduces a pre-fusion module in every decoder block that concatenates the refined global visual feature with previously generated word embeddings before masked self-attention, providing an early inter-modal interaction. This is followed by language masked self-attention and then cross-attention over the refined grid features. Ablation studies show the pre-fusion consistently improves all metrics compared to variants without it.

5. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Feeding many captions into a text-only QA model can exceed encoder length limits and incur quadratic self-attention cost if naively concatenated, hurting scalability and accuracy.
   method:  Adopt Fusion-in-Decoder (FiD): encode each question+caption pair separately and concatenate only the encoded representations as inputs to the decoder’s cross-attention. This avoids encoder length caps and is more efficient than Fusion-in-Encoder, enabling the model to exploit up to 100 captions. Empirically, FiD continues to improve with more captions while FiE saturates, and in the full plug-and-play pipeline (BLIP + UnifiedQAv2) yields state-of-the-art zero-shot VQA: e.g., PNP-VQA (11B PLM) surpasses Flamingo-80B by 8.5% on VQAv2 and PNP-VQA (738M PLM) outperforms FewVLMlarge on GQA by 9.1%.

7. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

8. [CV] Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models (2401.08541)
   problem: Next-patch pixel prediction can force the backbone to specialize in low-level details, hurting the quality of transferable features.
   method:  Insert a heavily-parameterized token-level MLP prediction head atop the ViT trunk that processes each patch independently before projecting to pixels. This head captures low-level signals needed for accurate pixel regression, keeping the trunk focused on higher-level, transferable representations. Experiments show the MLP head significantly improves downstream performance over having no head, a transformer head brings only marginal gains at much higher cost, and increasing head depth (more MLP blocks) is especially beneficial.

9. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

10. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

11. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

12. [MULTIMODAL] BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps (2005.04625)
   problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
   method:  Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] PinnerSage: Multi-Modal User Embedding Framework for Recommendations at Pinterest (2007.03634)
   problem: Nearest-neighbor candidate retrieval over billions of items must be accurate and cost/latency-efficient; naive indexing returns near-duplicates and is expensive to serve.
   method:  The system adopts HNSW for ANN indexing of pin embeddings, which outperforms LSH Orthoplex on cost/latency/recall. It refines the candidate pool by filtering near-duplicates and low-quality pins, and caches queries at the medoid pin-ID level (rather than raw embedding vectors). Together these choices significantly cut serving cost (e.g., ~60% vs LSH, ~50% via index refinement, ~75% from medoid caching) while maintaining high-recall retrieval.

15. [IR_REC] Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval (2203.08372)
   problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
   method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

16. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
   method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

17. [SPEECH] End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering (2511.09282)
   problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
   method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

18. [SPEECH] Curriculum Pre-training for End-to-End Speech Translation (2004.10093)
   problem: ASR pre-training alone does not endow the encoder with explicit sentence-level understanding of the source utterance beyond surface transcription.
   method:  Propose a Frame-based Masked Language Model (FMLM) for speech: use forced alignment to locate word-level time spans, randomly mask 15% of whole-word speech segments (filled with the utterance mean), and have the encoder predict the masked source word distribution from the average hidden states over the masked span. The loss is a KL-divergence to a target distribution over source BPE tokens and is computed at the N-th encoder layer (shared with ASR), explicitly training source-language understanding in the encoder.

19. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

20. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
   method:  Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.

21. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Robotic policies must fuse asynchronous, multi-frequency sensory streams (e.g., sparse images with high-rate proprioception/forces) and act nearly in continuous time. Common approaches batch observations into fixed windows or use open-loop primitives, failing to capture fast in-between dynamics and temporal structure.
   method:  Introduce InFuser, a hybrid continuous-time policy that treats control as the solution of a Neural Controlled Differential Equation. The model conditions on an image embedding and is driven by the higher-frequency sensor stream to evolve a continuous-time multisensory latent state, from which actions are decoded at any intermediate time. The initial action uses only currently available measurements, while subsequent actions integrate new high-rate observations via the CDE. Training uses adjoint-based backpropagation and behavior cloning over control functions. The architecture includes passing high-frequency measurements as a subvector of the latent and stacking an identity block in the CDE vector field, and it generalizes ODE/RNN/DMP parameterizations.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

Thought: The core of this method is speculative decoding (draft-verify with exact acceptance-rejection). I need to check whether the specific combination of parallel drafting with a lookahead mask, a shallow verifier gate, and Picard-iteration-style scheduling has been published.
Query: speculative decoding draft model parallel token verification large language model

Round 2/10.

Last search results:
1. Towards Optimal Multi-draft Speculative Decoding (International Conference on Learning Representations 2025, cites: 14)
   Large Language Models (LLMs) have become an indispensable part of natural language processing tasks. However, autoregressive sampling has become an efficiency bottleneck. Multi-Draft Speculative Decoding (MDSD) is a recent approach where, when generating each token, a small draft model generates multiple drafts, and the target LLM verifies them in parallel, ensuring that the final output conforms to the target model distribution. The two main design choices in MDSD are the draft sampling method and the verification algorithm. For a fixed draft sampling method, the optimal acceptance rate is a solution to an optimal transport problem, but the complexity of this problem makes it difficult to solve for the optimal acceptance rate and measure the gap between existing verification algorithms and the theoretical upper bound. This paper discusses the dual of the optimal transport problem, providing a way to efficiently compute the optimal acceptance rate. For the first time, we measure the theoretical upper bound of MDSD efficiency for vocabulary sizes in the thousands and quantify the gap between existing verification algorithms and this bound. We also compare different draft sampling methods based on their optimal acceptance rates. Our results show that the draft sampling method strongly influences the optimal acceptance rate, with sampling without replacement outperforming sampling with replacement. Additionally, existing verification algorithms do not reach the theoretical upper bound for both without replacement and with replacement sampling. Our findings suggest that carefully designed draft sampling methods can potentially improve the optimal acceptance rate and enable the development of verification algorithms that closely match the theoretical upper bound.

2. EasySpec: Layer-Parallel Speculative Decoding for Efficient Multi-GPU Utilization (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Speculative decoding is an effective and lossless method for Large Language Model (LLM) inference acceleration. It employs a smaller model to generate a draft token sequence, which is then verified by the original base model. In multi-GPU systems, inference latency can be further reduced through tensor parallelism (TP), while the optimal TP size of the draft model is typically smaller than that of the base model, leading to GPU idling during the drafting stage. We observe that such inefficiency stems from the sequential execution of layers, which is seemingly natural but actually unnecessary. Therefore, we propose EasySpec, a layer-parallel speculation strategy that optimizes the efficiency of multi-GPU utilization. EasySpec breaks the inter-layer data dependencies in the draft model, enabling multiple layers to run simultaneously across multiple devices as'fuzzy'speculation. After each drafting-and-verification iteration, the draft model's key-value cache is calibrated in a single forward pass, preventing long-term fuzzy-error accumulation at minimal additional latency. EasySpec is a training-free and plug-in method. We evaluated EasySpec on several mainstream open-source LLMs, using smaller versions of models from the same series as drafters. The results demonstrate that EasySpec can achieve a peak speedup of 4.17x compared to vanilla decoding, while preserving the original distributions of the base LLMs. Specifically, the drafting stage can be accelerated by up to 1.62x with a maximum speculation accuracy drop of only 7%. The code is available at https://github.com/Yize-Wu/EasySpec.

However, combining MoE with SD inflates GPU memory and aggravates CPU-GPU bandwidth contention during multi-token verification. Existing MoE offloading systems are SD-agnostic and do not address this bottleneck. We present SP-MoE, the first SD-aware expert-offloading and compute-communication pipelining framework. SP-MoE introduces: (1) speculative expert prefetching that exploits structural correspondence between the draft and target models to prefetch likely experts ahead of verification; (2) a cutoff-layer policy that bounds per-layer prefetch depth based on empirical profiles and an analytical latency model, guaranteeing just-in-time availability without overfetch; and (3) a pipelined runtime with asynchronous prefetch threads and batched I/O to hide loading latency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SP-MoE achieves a 1.07-3.5 times TPOT speedup over state-of-the-art methods across diverse datasets, environments, and MoE-based models.

4. Block Verification Accelerates Speculative Decoding (International Conference on Learning Representations 2024, cites: 21)
   Speculative decoding is an effective method for lossless acceleration of large language models during inference. It uses a fast model to draft a block of tokens which are then verified in parallel by the target model, and provides a guarantee that the output is distributed identically to a sample from the target model. In prior works, draft verification is performed independently token-by-token. Surprisingly, we show that this approach is not optimal. We propose Block Verification, a simple draft verification algorithm that verifies the entire block jointly and provides additional wall-clock speedup. We prove that the proposed mechanism is optimal in the expected number of tokens produced each iteration and specifically is never worse than the standard token-level verification. Empirically, block verification provides modest but consistent wall-clock speedups over the standard token verification algorithm of 5%-8% in a range of tasks and datasets. Given that block verification does not increase code complexity, maintains the strong lossless guarantee of the standard speculative decoding verification algorithm, cannot deteriorate performance, and, in fact, consistently improves it, it can be used as a good default in speculative decoding implementations.

SADDLE incorporates two key mechanisms: (1) an asynchronous speculative decoding pipeline that decouples DLM prediction and TLM verification to reduce idle time, and (2) an arithmetic intensity-aware operator scheduler that dynamically assigns operators to the most suitable hardware units. Experimental results show that SADDLE achieves average speedups of $\mathbf{2. 8 8} \times$ over a state-of-the-art GPU-only solution and $\mathbf{1. 7 1} \times$ over the best-performing GPU+PIM baseline.

10. Traversal Verification for Speculative Tree Decoding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 5)
   Speculative decoding is a promising approach for accelerating large language models. The primary idea is to use a lightweight draft model to speculate the output of the target model for multiple subsequent timesteps, and then verify them in parallel to determine whether the drafted tokens should be accepted or rejected. To enhance acceptance rates, existing frameworks typically construct token trees containing multiple candidates in each timestep. However, their reliance on token-level verification mechanisms introduces two critical limitations: First, the probability distribution of a sequence differs from that of individual tokens, leading to suboptimal acceptance length. Second, current verification schemes begin from the root node and proceed layer by layer in a top-down manner. Once a parent node is rejected, all its child nodes should be discarded, resulting in inefficient utilization of speculative candidates. This paper introduces Traversal Verification, a novel speculative decoding algorithm that fundamentally rethinks the verification paradigm through leaf-to-root traversal. Our approach considers the acceptance of the entire token sequence from the current node to the root, and preserves potentially valid subsequences that would be prematurely discarded by existing methods. We theoretically prove that the probability distribution obtained through Traversal Verification is identical to that of the target model, guaranteeing lossless inference while achieving substantial acceleration gains. Experimental results across different large language models and multiple tasks show that our method consistently improves acceptance length and throughput over existing methods.

1. PARD: Accelerating LLM Inference with Low-Cost PARallel Draft Model Adaptation (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 5)
   The autoregressive nature of large language models (LLMs) fundamentally limits inference speed, as each forward pass generates only a single token and is often bottlenecked by memory bandwidth. In this work, we propose \textbf{PARD (PARallel Draft)}, a novel speculative decoding method featuring \textit{target-independence} and \textit{parallel token prediction}. Specifically, PARD enables a single draft model to be applied across an entire family of target models without requiring separate training for each variant, thereby minimizing adaptation costs. Meanwhile, PARD substantially accelerates inference by predicting multiple future tokens within a single forward pass of the draft phase. To further reduce the training adaptation cost of PARD, we propose a COnditional Drop-token (COD) mechanism based on the integrity of prefix key-value states, enabling autoregressive draft models to be adapted into parallel draft models at low-cost. Our experiments show that the proposed COD method improves draft model training efficiency by \textbf{3$\times$} compared with traditional masked prediction training. On the \texttt{vLLM} inference framework, PARD achieves up to \textbf{3.67$\times$} speedup on LLaMA3.1-8B, reaching \textbf{264.88} tokens per second, which is \textbf{1.15$\times$} faster than EAGLE-3. Our code is available at https://github.com/AMD-AIG-AIMA/PARD.

2. Efficient LLM Inference over Heterogeneous Edge Networks with Speculative Decoding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)
   Large language model (LLM) inference at the network edge is a promising serving paradigm that leverages distributed edge resources to run inference near users and enhance privacy. Existing edge-based LLM inference systems typically adopt autoregressive decoding (AD), which only generates one token per forward pass. This iterative process, compounded by the limited computational resources of edge nodes, results in high serving latency and constrains the system's ability to support multiple users under growing demands.To address these challenges, we propose a speculative decoding (SD)-based LLM serving framework that deploys small and large models across heterogeneous edge nodes to collaboratively deliver inference services. Specifically, the small model rapidly generates draft tokens that the large model verifies in parallel, enabling multi-token generation per forward pass and thus reducing serving latency. To improve resource utilization of edge nodes, we incorporate pipeline parallelism to overlap drafting and verification across multiple inference tasks. Based on this framework, we analyze and derive a comprehensive latency model incorporating both communication and inference latency. Then, we formulate a joint optimization problem for speculation length, task batching, and wireless communication resource allocation to minimize total serving latency. To address this problem, we derive the closed-form solutions for wireless communication resource allocation, and develop a dynamic programming algorithm for joint batching and speculation control strategies. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves lower serving latency compared to AD-based serving systems. In addition,the proposed joint optimization method delivers up to 44.9% latency reduction compared to benchmark schemes.

3. Speculative Decoding via Early-exiting for Faster LLM Inference with Thompson Sampling Control Mechanism (Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics 2024, cites: 35)
   The recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have been extraordinary, yet the escalating inference costs associated with them present challenges in real-world applications. Specifically, EESD utilizes a segment of the LLM to generate draft tokens, incorporating Early-exiting structures after the first N layers. The original LLM is then employed to validate these draft tokens through a single forward pass, and thus guarantees that the final output text maintains a distribution consistent with vanilla auto-regressive decoding. The experimental results on both 13B and 70B models demonstrate that our approach decodes tokens at a markedly accelerated rate compared to prior methods, showing the effectiveness of our approach.

DART predicts logits for multiple future masked positions in parallel within a single forward pass based on hidden states of the target model, thereby eliminating autoregressive rollouts in the draft model while preserving a lightweight design. 6. SpecExec: Massively Parallel Speculative Decoding for Interactive LLM Inference on Consumer Devices (Neural Information Processing Systems 2024, cites: 55)

However, most of these works implicitly design their algorithms for high-end datacenter hardware. In this work, we ask the opposite question: how fast can we run LLMs on consumer machines? Consumer GPUs can no longer fit the largest available models (50B+ parameters) and must offload them to RAM or SSD. When running with offloaded parameters, the inference engine can process batches of hundreds or thousands of tokens at the same time as just one token, making it a natural fit for speculative decoding. We propose SpecExec (Speculative Execution), a simple parallel decoding method that can generate up to 20 tokens per target model iteration for popular LLM families. Using SpecExec, we demonstrate inference of 50B+ parameter LLMs on consumer GPUs with RAM offloading at 4-6 tokens per second with 4-bit quantization or 2-3 tokens per second with 16-bit weights.

However, very few works have fundamentally changed the generation pattern: each token needs one forward pass and one KV cache. Extensive evaluations show that HAMburger reduces the KV cache computation by up to 2$\times$ and achieves up to 2$\times$ TPS, while maintaining quality in both short- and long-context tasks. The results demonstrate that EasySpec can achieve a peak speedup of 4.17x compared to vanilla decoding, while preserving the original distributions of the base LLMs. Specifically, the drafting stage can be accelerated by up to 1.62x with a maximum speculation accuracy drop of only 7%. The code is available at https://github.com/Yize-Wu/EasySpec.

3. Efficient LLM Inference over Heterogeneous Edge Networks with Speculative Decoding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)
   Large language model (LLM) inference at the network edge is a promising serving paradigm that leverages distributed edge resources to run inference near users and enhance privacy. Existing edge-based LLM inference systems typically adopt autoregressive decoding (AD), which only generates one token per forward pass. This iterative process, compounded by the limited computational resources of edge nodes, results in high serving latency and constrains the system's ability to support multiple users under growing demands.To address these challenges, we propose a speculative decoding (SD)-based LLM serving framework that deploys small and large models across heterogeneous edge nodes to collaboratively deliver inference services. Specifically, the small model rapidly generates draft tokens that the large model verifies in parallel, enabling multi-token generation per forward pass and thus reducing serving latency. To improve resource utilization of edge nodes, we incorporate pipeline parallelism to overlap drafting and verification across multiple inference tasks. Based on this framework, we analyze and derive a comprehensive latency model incorporating both communication and inference latency. Then, we formulate a joint optimization problem for speculation length, task batching, and wireless communication resource allocation to minimize total serving latency. To address this problem, we derive the closed-form solutions for wireless communication resource allocation, and develop a dynamic programming algorithm for joint batching and speculation control strategies. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves lower serving latency compared to AD-based serving systems. In addition,the proposed joint optimization method delivers up to 44.9% latency reduction compared to benchmark schemes.

4. Nightjar: Dynamic Adaptive Speculative Decoding for Large Language Models Serving (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates LLM inference by verifying draft tokens in parallel. However, this method presents a critical trade-off: it improves throughput in low-load, memory-bound systems but degrades performance in high-load, compute-bound environments due to verification overhead. Existing speculative decoding methods use fixed lengths and cannot adapt to workload changes or decide when to stop speculation. The cost of restarting speculative inference also remains unquantified. Under high load, the benefit of speculation diminishes, while retaining the draft model reduces KV-cache capacity, limiting batch size and degrading throughput. To overcome this, we propose Nightjar, a resource-aware adaptive speculative framework. It first adjusts to the request load by dynamically selecting the optimal speculative length for different batch sizes. Crucially, Nightjar proactively disables speculative decoding when the MAB planner determines that speculation is no longer beneficial, and during the disabled phase, offloads the draft model to the CPU only under GPU memory pressure. This reclaims memory for the KV cache, thereby facilitating larger batch sizes and maximizing overall system throughput. Experiments show that Nightjar achieves average 27.29% higher throughput and up to 20.18% lower latency compared to standard speculative decoding under dynamic request arrival rates in real-time LLM serving scenarios.

   Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) exhibit remarkable generative capabilities, but their inference throughput is limited by the autoregressive decoding process, which generates only one token per iteration. Speculative decoding mitigates this bottleneck by using a lightweight draft language model (DLM) to generate multiple draft tokens, which are then verified in parallel by a more accurate target language model (TLM). To accommodate the differing computational patterns of the DLM and TLM, prior work has leveraged heterogeneous systems combining xPUs and processing-in-memory (PIM) units to offload compute- and memory-intensive operators, respectively. However, existing systems often adopt a fixed draft sequence length, leading to excessive rejection of draft tokens during verification-especially under large-batch scenarios-resulting in redundant computation and reduced efficiency. This paper proposes a runtime adaptive draft length adjustment technique that dynamically tailors the draft length for each request by monitoring cumulative acceptance probabilities, thereby minimizing the generation and verification of invalid tokens. Yet, integrating adaptive draft lengths into existing PIM-enabled heterogeneous systems introduces two new challenges: (1) sequential execution of the DLM and TLM becomes inefficient due to synchronization bubbles caused by request-wise variability in draft lengths, and (2) static operator mappings become suboptimal as draft length variability alters operator arithmetic intensities dynamically. To address these issues, we introduce SADDLE, a PIM-enabled heterogeneous system designed to exploit adaptive draft lengths effectively. SADDLE incorporates two key mechanisms: (1) an asynchronous speculative decoding pipeline that decouples DLM prediction and TLM verification to reduce idle time, and (2) an arithmetic intensity-aware operator scheduler that dynamically assigns operators to the most suitable hardware units. Experimental results show that SADDLE achieves average speedups of $\mathbf{2. 8 8} \times$ over a state-of-the-art GPU-only solution and $\mathbf{1. 7 1} \times$ over the best-performing GPU+PIM baseline.

6. Clover: Regressive Lightweight Speculative Decoding with Sequential Knowledge (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 11)
   Large language models (LLMs) suffer from low efficiency as the mismatch between the requirement of auto-regressive decoding and the design of most contemporary GPUs. Specifically, billions to trillions of parameters must be loaded to the GPU cache through its limited memory bandwidth for computation, but only a small batch of tokens is actually computed. Consequently, the GPU spends most of its time on memory transfer instead of computation. Recently, parallel decoding, a type of speculative decoding algorithms, is becoming more popular and has demonstrated impressive efficiency improvement in generation. It introduces extra decoding heads to large models, enabling them to predict multiple subsequent tokens simultaneously and verify these candidate continuations in a single decoding step. However, this approach deviates from the training objective of next token prediction used during pre-training, resulting in a low hit rate for candidate tokens. In this paper, we propose a new speculative decoding algorithm, Clover, which integrates sequential knowledge into the parallel decoding process. This enhancement improves the hit rate of speculators and thus boosts the overall efficiency. Clover transmits the sequential knowledge from pre-speculated tokens via the Regressive Connection, then employs an Attention Decoder to integrate these speculated tokens. Additionally, Clover incorporates an Augmenting Block that modifies the hidden states to better align with the purpose of speculative generation rather than next token prediction. The experiment results demonstrate that Clover outperforms the baseline by up to 91% on Baichuan-Small and 146% on Baichuan-Large, respectively, and exceeds the performance of the previously top-performing method, Medusa, by up to 37% on Baichuan-Small and 57% on Baichuan-Large, respectively.

A token-level FSM guarantees syntactic validity, and a serving-layer primitive updates the KV cache for each copy span via a single parallel-prefill forward rather than $N$ autoregressive steps -- sharing the parallel-forward kernel of speculative decoding but with input tokens as the draft and program-enforced acceptance replacing probabilistic verification. We report an upper-bound analysis that requires no end-to-end training. (i) Kernel speedup: on Qwen2.5-{1.5B, 7B}, copying $N$ tokens via parallel prefill is $6.8\times$--$303\times$ faster than autoregressive ($N \in [8, 512]$, A100 80GB bf16). (ii) Copy ceiling: on ProbeEdit and HumanEvalPack-Fix (Py/JS), $74$--$98\%$ of gold tokens are reachable under the line-level primitive; composed with the empirical kernel over each corpus's span histogram this yields a closed-form wall-clock bound of $29.0\times / 3.4\times / 4.2\times$ ($13.0\times$ pooled). A token-level extension reaches $91$--$99\%$ coverage with $4.5\times$--$6.5\times$ floors. (iii) Pipeline losslessness: oracle programs round-trip through the deterministic resolver on all $482$ cases, localizing any downstream failure to span selection rather than the mechanism. A perturbation study shows pooled EM drops from $100\%$ to $15.48\%$ under off-by-one noise. A fine-tuning pilot on Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B lifts HEvalFix-Py EM from $0/33$ (untrained) to $12$--$17\%$, a learnability signal, not a production selector. Batched-serving integration and multi-file coverage are scoped as follow-up.

4. Jakiro: Boosting Speculative Decoding with Decoupled Multi-Head via MoE (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)

However, the limited capacity of the draft model often necessitates tree-based sampling to improve prediction accuracy, where multiple candidates are generated at each step. We identify a key limitation in this approach: the candidates at the same step are derived from the same representation, limiting diversity and reducing overall effectiveness. To address this, we propose Jakiro, leveraging Mixture of Experts (MoE), where independent experts generate diverse predictions, effectively decoupling correlations among candidates. Furthermore, we introduce a hybrid inference strategy, combining autoregressive decoding for initial tokens with parallel decoding for subsequent stages, and enhance the latter with contrastive mechanism in features to improve accuracy. Our method significantly boosts prediction accuracy and achieves higher inference speedups. Extensive experiments across diverse models validate the effectiveness and robustness of our approach, establishing a new SOTA in speculative decoding. Our codes are available at https://github.com/haiduo/Jakiro.

5. Kangaroo: Lossless Self-Speculative Decoding for Accelerating LLMs via Double Early Exiting (Neural Information Processing Systems 2024, cites: 28)

However, the conventional approach of training separate draft model to achieve a satisfactory token acceptance rate can be costly and impractical. In this paper, we propose a novel self-speculative decoding framework Kangaroo with double early exiting strategy, which leverages the shallow sub-network and the LM Head of the well-trained target LLM to construct a self-drafting model. Then, the self-verification stage only requires computing the remaining layers over the early-exited hidden states in parallel. To bridge the representation gap between the sub-network and the full model, we train a lightweight and efficient adapter module on top of the sub-network. One significant challenge that comes with the proposed method is that the inference latency of the self-draft model may no longer be negligible compared to the big model. To boost the token acceptance rate while minimizing the latency of the self-drafting model, we introduce an additional early exiting mechanism for both single-sequence and the tree decoding scenarios. Specifically, we dynamically halt the small model’s subsequent prediction during the drafting phase once the confidence level for the current step falls below a certain threshold. This approach reduces unnecessary computations and improves overall efficiency. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate our effectiveness, where Kangaroo achieves walltime speedups up to 2.04 × , outperforming Medusa-1 with 88.7% fewer additional parameters. The code for Kangaroo is available at https://github.com/Equationliu/Kangaroo .

While speculative decoding has emerged as a promising acceleration technique through its draft-then-verify framework, current implementations suffer from two critical limitations: (1) mutual waiting problem caused by sequential dependencies between draft generation and verification phases, and (2) constrained token acceptance rates where retrieval-based drafting methods under-perform in general domains while models-based drafting approaches show reduced efficacy in knowledge-intensive scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose Talon, a novel parallel inference architecture featuring two key innovations: (1) **a novel asynchronous execution paradigm** that decouples draft generation from verification, effectively eliminating synchronization bottlenecks, and (2) **an adaptive hybrid drafting strategy** that dynamically combines model-based and retrieval-based approaches to improve token acceptance rates across diverse domains. Extensive evaluations across standard benchmarks (MT-Bench, HumanEval, GSM8K, Alpaca, CNN/DM) demonstrate Talon's exceptional performance, achieving 4.04x–6.52x acceleration across multiple model families including Vicuna, Deepseek, and LLaMA series. These results represent a significant advancement over existing speculative decoding methods (EAGLE 1-3, Hydra, Medusa, Lookahead, SPS, and PLD), establishing a new paradigm for speculative decoding.

7. Falcon: Faster and Parallel Inference of Large Language Models through Enhanced Semi-Autoregressive Drafting and Custom-Designed Decoding Tree (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2024, cites: 20)
   Striking an optimal balance between minimal drafting latency and high speculation accuracy to enhance the inference speed of Large Language Models remains a significant challenge in speculative decoding. In this paper, we introduce Falcon, an innovative semi-autoregressive speculative decoding framework fashioned to augment both the drafter's parallelism and output quality. Falcon incorporates the Coupled Sequential Glancing Distillation technique, which fortifies inter-token dependencies within the same block, leading to increased speculation accuracy. We offer a comprehensive theoretical analysis to illuminate the underlying mechanisms. Additionally, we introduce a Custom-Designed Decoding Tree, which permits the drafter to generate multiple tokens in a single forward pass and accommodates multiple forward passes as needed, thereby boosting the number of drafted tokens and significantly improving the overall acceptance rate. Comprehensive evaluations on benchmark datasets such as MT-Bench, HumanEval, and GSM8K demonstrate Falcon's superior acceleration capabilities. The framework achieves a lossless speedup ratio ranging from 2.91x to 3.51x when tested on the Vicuna and LLaMA2-Chat model series. These results outstrip existing speculative decoding methods for LLMs, including Eagle, Medusa, Lookahead, SPS, and PLD, while maintaining a compact drafter architecture equivalent to merely two Transformer layers.

MARS adds no architectural modifications, no extra parameters, and produces a single model that can still be called exactly like the original AR model with no performance degradation. Unlike speculative decoding, which maintains a separate draft model alongside the target, or multi-head approaches such as Medusa, which attach additional prediction heads, MARS requires only continued training on existing instruction data. When generating one token per forward pass, MARS matches or exceeds the AR baseline on six standard benchmarks. When allowed to accept multiple tokens per step, it maintains baseline-level accuracy while achieving 1.5-1.7x throughput. We further develop a block-level KV caching strategy for batch inference, achieving up to 1.71x wall-clock speedup over AR with KV cache on Qwen2.5-7B. Finally, MARS supports real-time speed adjustment via confidence thresholding: under high request load, the serving system can increase throughput on the fly without swapping models or restarting, providing a practical latency-quality knob for deployment.

To further reduce the training adaptation cost of PARD, we propose a COnditional Drop-token (COD) mechanism based on the integrity of prefix key-value states, enabling autoregressive draft models to be adapted into parallel draft models at low-cost. Our experiments show that the proposed COD method improves draft model training efficiency by \textbf{3$\times$} compared with traditional masked prediction training. On the \texttt{vLLM} inference framework, PARD achieves up to \textbf{3.67$\times$} speedup on LLaMA3.1-8B, reaching \textbf{264.88} tokens per second, which is \textbf{1.15$\times$} faster than EAGLE-3. messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token needs a full
large-model forward pass; how can a small draft model propose
tokens in parallel that the large model verifies without quality
loss?


### Proposed method
We propose Parallel Draft–Verify Decoding (PDVD): a small draft model D proposes a block of the next B tokens in parallel, and the large target model L verifies and commits a maximal prefix of that block, guaranteeing the exact output that L would have produced under the chosen decoding policy (greedy or stochastic). At each step, we maintain L’s KV cache for the accepted prefix. D is a lightweight transformer trained to approximate L’s next-token distribution and, crucially, to predict the next B positions simultaneously using a lookahead masking scheme so its B predictions can be produced in one pass.

Drafting: Given prefix x1:t, we append B masked slots and run D once with a position-specific lookahead mask that prevents the masked slots from attending to each other, letting every future position see the same prefix but not cross-talk with other future positions. D outputs logits (or top-m lists) for positions t+1..t+B in parallel; we sample or select candidates from these logits according to the downstream decoding policy (e.g., greedy or temperature sampling) to form a draft block ŷ1:B.

Verification: We run L autoregressively over the draft block reusing the KV cache, but stop as soon as we encounter the first position j where D’s draft and L’s policy disagree. For greedy decoding, we accept all ŷ1:(j−1) that match L and set yj to L’s argmax; for stochastic decoding, we perform acceptance–rejection at each position i using the standard probability ratio test min(1, pL(yi|x, y< i)/pD(yi|x, y< i)), which yields exact samples from L. To save compute when a mismatch is likely, we add a shallow verifier gate g that inspects early-layer activations and D’s logits to predict rejection; if g flags a likely mismatch at position i, we abort the deep layers for that position and mark it as a rejection early, while still running the full L pass for any token we finally commit—preserving exactness.

Scheduling and pipelining: While L verifies the current block, D immediately drafts the next block from the (temporarily) extended context, keeping both models busy. Across a batch of sequences, we verify blocks in a strided, GPU-friendly manner. To further increase parallelism without changing correctness, maintain a cache of tentative tokens across future steps and iterate in sweeps: each sweep lets L verify only its assigned positions (earliest unverified positions across requests) using cached guesses elsewhere; after the sweep, we write back accepted tokens and repeat. This converges to the same sequence as fully sequential decoding, yet enables high hardware utilization via batched verification.

Training D and the verifier gate: Train D via distillation from L on next-token prediction and a blockwise objective: for randomly chosen prefixes, predict the next B tokens with the lookahead mask so every position learns to rely solely on the prefix. Train the shallow gate g to predict “accept vs reject” using features from early L layers and D’s logits, with a loss that heavily penalizes false accepts; at inference, g only short-circuits expensive verification on tokens that would be rejected, never on tokens we ultimately commit.

### Rationale
This design eliminates quality loss by committing tokens only after L has validated them, and by using exact acceptance–rejection for stochastic decoding. The small D amortizes the expensive large-model passes: if the average accepted-prefix length per draft is r·B (r = acceptance rate), then L performs one forward sweep per r·B emitted tokens instead of per token, yielding a speedup of roughly 1/r in the large-model compute, while D’s parallel draft is cheap and overlapped. The verifier gate further reduces wasted large-model work by aborting deep-layer computation early on blocks that will be rejected, without changing any committed outputs.

The lookahead masking allows D to produce multiple future positions in a single pass without causal leakage between those positions, which is what makes parallel drafting both valid and efficient. The iterative, cache-based scheduling maintains high device utilization and mirrors a fixed-point view of sequential decoding: multiple tentative positions are updated and corrected until they match L’s true trajectory, but tokens are only emitted once L has fully verified them—preserving exact equivalence to L’s outputs.
